summary: Jeff Atwood documents his search for a new laptop that breaks from the corporate ThinkPad mold, establishing specific criteria including Core Duo CPU, sub-5-pound weight, dedicated graphics for Vista, and optional optical drive removal. After evaluating six options including ThinkPad, Samsung, MacBook Pro, Dell, and Sony, he selects the Asus W3J as the winner. The Asus hits all his requirements while offering a clean aluminum design and a swappable bay system. He notes that Asus manufactures Apple's laptops, which shows in the build quality, and shares news of silent spec upgrades for June shipments.
key_points: ThinkPads represent boring corporate conformity - carrying one signals you don't care about your tools
The ideal 2006 laptop needs Core Duo, dedicated graphics for Vista, under 5 pounds, and no mandatory optical drive
MacBook Pro hardware is excellent but wasted if you're only going to run Windows on it
Repeatedly choosing Dell represents 'a catastrophic failure of imagination' despite good value
Asus W3J wins by hitting all criteria plus offering a swappable bay for optical drive, battery, or hard drive
money_quotes: Thinkpads are uninspiring. They're the gray flannel suit of the IT industry.
Every time I pulled the T43 out of my bag, I felt like I was advertising the fact that I didn't give a damn.
Really, who uses optical drives any more? That's an extra half-pound I'd rather not carry around.
We live in a world of platform choices; repeatedly choosing a Dell is a catastrophic failure of imagination.
I don't trust Sony for drivers and support; their stuff is always pretty but vapid.
Asus is the OEM who manufactures Apple's laptops, and it shows.
key_insight: The laptop you carry is a statement about whether you care about your craft, and settling for boring corporate defaults signals indifference.
